([Slovene](/wiki/Slovene_language "Slovene language"): *Univerza v Ljubljani*, pronounced [[uniʋɛ́ːɾza w ljubljàːni]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Slovene "Help:IPA/Slovene"), [Latin](/wiki/Latin_language "Latin language"): *Universitas Labacensis*), abbreviated **UL**, is the oldest and largest [university](/wiki/University "University") in [Slovenia](/wiki/Slovenia "Slovenia"). It has approximately 38,000 enrolled students.[[8]](#cite_note-8) The university has 23 faculties and three art academies with approximately 4,000 teaching and research staff, assisted by approximately 2,000 technical and administrative staff. The University of Ljubljana offers programs in the humanities, sciences, and technology, as well as in medicine, dentistry, and veterinary science.

The **University of Ljubljana** ([Slovene](/wiki/Slovene_language "Slovene language"): *Univerza v Ljubljani*, pronounced [[uniʋɛ́ːɾza w ljubljàːni]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Slovene "Help:IPA/Slovene"), [Latin](/wiki/Latin_language "Latin language"): *Universitas Labacensis*), abbreviated **UL**, is the oldest and largest [university](/wiki/University "University") in [Slovenia](/wiki/Slovenia "Slovenia"). It has approximately 38,000 enrolled students.[[8]](#cite_note-8) The university has 23 faculties and three art academies with approximately 4,000 teaching and research staff, assisted by approximately 2,000 technical and administrative staff. The University of Ljubljana offers programs in the humanities, sciences, and technology, as well as in medicine, dentistry, and veterinary science.

The university was founded in the centre of Ljubljana, where the central university building and the majority of its faculties are located. Since then, newer buildings have been constructed in the suburbs of the city.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Ljubljana&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Beginnings

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Ljubljana&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Beginnings")]

Although certain academies (notably of [philosophy](/wiki/Philosophy "Philosophy") and [theology](/wiki/Theology "Theology")) were established as [Jesuit](/wiki/Jesuit "Jesuit") higher education in what is now Slovenia as early as the seventeenth century, the first university was founded in 1810 under the *Écoles centrales* of the [French imperial](/wiki/First_French_Empire "First French Empire") administration of the [Illyrian provinces](/wiki/Illyrian_provinces "Illyrian provinces"). The chancellor of the university in Ljubljana during the French period was [Joseph Walland](/w/index.php?title=Joseph_Walland&action=edit&redlink=1 "Joseph Walland (page does not exist)") (a.k.a. [Jožef Balant](/w/index.php?title=Jo%C5%BEef_Balant&action=edit&redlink=1 "Jožef Balant (page does not exist)") [[sl](https://sl.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jo%C5%BEef_Balant "sl:Jožef Balant")], 1763–1834), born in [Upper Carniola](/wiki/Upper_Carniola "Upper Carniola"). That university was disbanded in 1813, when [Austria](/wiki/Austrian_Empire "Austrian Empire") regained territorial control and reestablished the Imperial Royal Lyceum of Ljubljana as a higher-education institution.

### Quest for a national university

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Ljubljana&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Quest for a national university")]

During the second half of the 19th century, several political claims for the establishment of a [Slovene-language](/wiki/Slovene_language "Slovene language") university in Ljubljana were made. They gained momentum in the [fin de siècle](/wiki/Fin_de_si%C3%A8cle "Fin de siècle") era, when a considerable number of renowned [Slovene](/wiki/Slovenes "Slovenes") academians worked throughout [Central Europe](/wiki/Central_Europe "Central Europe"), while ever more numerous Slovenian students were enrolled in foreign-language universities of the [Austro-Hungarian Empire](/wiki/Austro-Hungarian_Empire "Austro-Hungarian Empire"), particularly in the [Austrian](/wiki/Austria "Austria") and [Czech lands](/wiki/Czech_lands "Czech lands"). Notable examples are the [Charles University](/wiki/Charles_University "Charles University") in [Prague](/wiki/Prague "Prague") and the [University of Olomouc](/wiki/Palack%C3%BD_University_Olomouc "Palacký University Olomouc"), the latter of which Slovene [philosopher](/wiki/Philosopher "Philosopher") [Franc Samuel Karpe](/wiki/Franc_Samuel_Karpe "Franc Samuel Karpe") became the [chancellor](/wiki/Chancellor_(education) "Chancellor (education)") of in 1781. In the 1890s, a unified board for the establishment of a Slovenian university was founded, with [Ivan Hribar](/wiki/Ivan_Hribar "Ivan Hribar"), [Henrik Tuma](/w/index.php?title=Henrik_Tuma&action=edit&redlink=1 "Henrik Tuma (page does not exist)"), and [Aleš Ušeničnik](/wiki/Ale%C5%A1_U%C5%A1eni%C4%8Dnik "Aleš Ušeničnik") as its main leaders. In 1898, the [Carniolan](/wiki/Carniola "Carniola") regional parliament established a scholarship[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] for all students who were planning a [habilitation](/wiki/Habilitation "Habilitation") under the condition that they would accept a post at Ljubljana University when founded. In this way, a list of suitable members of faculty started to emerge.

Nevertheless, unfavorable political circumstances prevented the establishment of the university until the fall of the Austro-Hungarian Empire. With the establishment of the short-lived [State of Slovenes, Croats and Serbs](/wiki/State_of_Slovenes,_Croats_and_Serbs "State of Slovenes, Croats and Serbs") and the [Kingdom of Serbs, Croats and Slovenes](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Yugoslavia "Kingdom of Yugoslavia") (Kingdom of Yugoslavia) in 1918, the founding of the university became possible. On November 23, 1918, the first meeting of the Founding Board of Ljubljana University was called, presided over by [Mihajlo Rostohar](/wiki/Mihajlo_Rostohar "Mihajlo Rostohar"), professor of [psychology](/wiki/Psychology "Psychology") at the [Charles University](/wiki/Charles_University "Charles University") in [Prague](/wiki/Prague "Prague"). Together with [Danilo Majaron](/w/index.php?title=Danilo_Majaron&action=edit&redlink=1 "Danilo Majaron (page does not exist)"), Rostohar convinced the central government of the [Kingdom of Serbs, Croats and Slovenes](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Yugoslavia "Kingdom of Yugoslavia") in [Belgrade](/wiki/Belgrade "Belgrade") to pass a bill formally establishing the university. The bill was passed on July 2, 1919; in late August, the first professors were appointed, and on September 18, the full professors established the University Council, thus starting the normal functioning of the institution. The first lectures started on December 3 of the same year.

### First decades

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Ljubljana&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: First decades")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9c/Univerza_v_Ljubljani_1929.jpg/250px-Univerza_v_Ljubljani_1929.jpg)](/wiki/File:Univerza_v_Ljubljani_1929.jpg)

University administrative building in 1929

In 1919, the university comprised five faculties: law, philosophy, technology, theology and medicine. The seat of the university was in the central [Congress Square](/wiki/Congress_Square "Congress Square") of Ljubljana in a building that had served as the State Mansion of Carniola from 1902 to 1918. The building was first designed in 1902 by [Jan Vladimír Hráský](/wiki/Jan_Vladim%C3%ADr_Hr%C3%A1sk%C3%BD "Jan Vladimír Hráský"), and was later remodelled by a [Czech](/wiki/Czech_people "Czech people") architect from [Vienna](/wiki/Vienna "Vienna"), [Josip Hudetz](/w/index.php?title=Josip_Hudetz&action=edit&redlink=1 "Josip Hudetz (page does not exist)").

In the mid-1920s, the university was renamed the "[King Alexander](/wiki/Alexander_I_of_Yugoslavia "Alexander I of Yugoslavia") University in Ljubljana" (*Universitas Alexandrina Labacensis*) and continued to grow despite financial troubles and constant pressure from Yugoslav governments’ [centralist](/wiki/Centralist "Centralist") policies. In 1941, [Jože Plečnik](/wiki/Jo%C5%BEe_Ple%C4%8Dnik "Jože Plečnik")'s [National and University Library](/wiki/National_and_University_Library_of_Slovenia "National and University Library of Slovenia") was completed, as one of the major infrastructure projects of the university in the interwar period.

After the [invasion of Yugoslavia](/wiki/Invasion_of_Yugoslavia "Invasion of Yugoslavia") in April 1941, the university continued to function under the [Italian and Nazi German occupation](/wiki/Province_of_Ljubljana "Province of Ljubljana"), despite numerous problems and interference in its autonomous operation. Several professors were arrested or deported to [Nazi concentration camps](/wiki/Nazi_concentration_camps "Nazi concentration camps") and large numbers of students joined either the [Liberation Front of the Slovenian People](/wiki/Liberation_Front_of_the_Slovenian_People "Liberation Front of the Slovenian People") or the [Slovenian Home Guard](/wiki/Slovenian_Home_Guard "Slovenian Home Guard").

### 1945 and later

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Ljubljana&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 1945 and later")]

Following the end of the Second World War, the first and only foreigner elected to hold the office of chancellor was the [Czech](/wiki/Czechs "Czechs") professor Alois Král, who had lectured at Faculty of Technical Sciences since 1920 and also held the position of dean thereof four times.[[9]](#cite_note-9) After the establishment of Communist [Yugoslavia](/wiki/Socialist_Federal_Republic_of_Yugoslavia "Socialist Federal Republic of Yugoslavia") in 1945, the university was again put under political pressure[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]: numerous professors were dismissed[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*], some were arrested and tried[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*], and the theological faculty was excluded from the university.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Some of the most brilliant students emigrated[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]. Nevertheless, the university maintained its educational role and regained a limited degree of autonomy from the mid-1950s onward. It suffered a serious setback in autonomy from the mid-1970s to the early 1980s, when some professors were again dismissed by the authorities[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]. In 1979 it was renamed "[Edvard Kardelj](/wiki/Edvard_Kardelj "Edvard Kardelj") University in Ljubljana" after the Communist leader. In 1990, with the fall of Yugoslavia, it was regiven its original name.

Later on, some new, modern buildings and a small scale campus were constructed in other parts of the city ([Bežigrad](/wiki/Be%C5%BEigrad "Bežigrad"), [Vič](/wiki/Vi%C4%8D "Vič"), [Brdo](/w/index.php?title=Brdo,_Ljubljana&action=edit&redlink=1 "Brdo, Ljubljana (page does not exist)")).

### Libraries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Ljubljana&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Libraries")]

The University of Ljubljana has two [university libraries](/wiki/University_library "University library"). The [National and University Library of Slovenia](/wiki/National_and_University_Library_of_Slovenia "National and University Library of Slovenia") is the [national library](/wiki/National_library "National library") of Slovenia and the university library of the University of Ljubljana. It contains about 1,307,000 books and numerous text, visual and multimedia resources.

Another university library is the [Central Technological Library](/w/index.php?title=Central_Technological_Library&action=edit&redlink=1 "Central Technological Library (page does not exist)"), which is also the national library and information hub of natural sciences and technology. There are over 30 libraries at individual faculties, departments, and institutes of the University of Ljubljana. The largest among them are the [Central Humanist Library](/w/index.php?title=Central_Humanist_Library_of_the_Faculty_of_Arts&action=edit&redlink=1 "Central Humanist Library of the Faculty of Arts (page does not exist)") in the field of [humanities](/wiki/Humanities "Humanities"), the [Central Economic Library](/w/index.php?title=Central_Economic_Library&action=edit&redlink=1 "Central Economic Library (page does not exist)") in the field of [economics](/wiki/Economics "Economics"), the [Central Medical Library](/w/index.php?title=Central_Medical_Library&action=edit&redlink=1 "Central Medical Library (page does not exist)") in the field of [medical sciences](/wiki/Medical_sciences "Medical sciences"), and the [Libraries of the Biotechnical Faculty](/w/index.php?title=Libraries_of_the_Biotechnical_Faculty&action=edit&redlink=1 "Libraries of the Biotechnical Faculty (page does not exist)") in the field of [biology](/wiki/Biology "Biology") and [biotechnology](/wiki/Biotechnology "Biotechnology").[[11]](#cite_note-11)

The university operates an art gallery, open since 18 June 2012.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

## Academics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Ljubljana&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Academics")]

The University of Ljubljana practices research in [science](/wiki/Science "Science") and the [arts](/wiki/Arts "Arts"), such as the [humanities](/wiki/Humanities "Humanities"), [social sciences](/wiki/Social_sciences "Social sciences"), [linguistics](/wiki/Linguistics "Linguistics"), [arts](/wiki/Arts "Arts"), [medicine](/wiki/Medicine "Medicine"), [natural sciences](/wiki/Natural_sciences "Natural sciences") and [technology](/wiki/Technology "Technology").

The University of Ljubljana used to house the permanent seat of the International Association for Political Science Students (IAPSS), an international academic group consisting of 10,000 political science graduate and undergraduate students worldwide from 2004 to 2013. In March 2013 the Permanent Seat was relocated to Nijmegen (Netherlands).[[13]](#cite_note-13)

The University of Ljubljana was ranked 601-650 in [QS World University Rankings](/wiki/QS_World_University_Rankings "QS World University Rankings") 2023.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

## Notable people

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Ljubljana&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Notable people")]

Further information: [List of University of Ljubljana people](/wiki/List_of_University_of_Ljubljana_people "List of University of Ljubljana people")

* [Slavoj Žižek](/wiki/Slavoj_%C5%BDi%C5%BEek "Slavoj Žižek") - Slovenian psychoanalytic philosopher, cultural critic, and Hegelian Marxist. He attended Ljubljana and attained a Master of Arts in philosophy in 1975,[[15]](#cite_note-15) and is a senior researcher at the Institute for Sociology and Philosophy at the University of Ljubljana.
* [Marko Bošnjak (judge)](/wiki/Marko_Bo%C5%A1njak_(judge) "Marko Bošnjak (judge)") - (born 12 March 1974) is a [Slovenian](/wiki/Slovenia "Slovenia") jurist, judge of the [European Court of Human Rights](/wiki/European_Court_of_Human_Rights "European Court of Human Rights"), attorney and academic.
* [Matjaž Gams](/wiki/Matja%C5%BE_Gams "Matjaž Gams") - Editor in chief for *Informatica*.[[16]](#cite_note-16) Member of Slovenian Academy of Engineering[[17]](#cite_note-17) and [European Academy of Sciences and Arts](/wiki/European_Academy_of_Sciences_and_Arts "European Academy of Sciences and Arts")[[18]](#cite_note-18)
